Title: Permanent cultural house strengthens Dusun Labuk identity, visibility
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Dusun Labuk community is gaining greater visibility and recognition with the establishment of a permanent cultural house at Koisaan Cultural Village (KCV) in Hongkod Koisaan, Penampang.
The ethnic group, whose members are mainly found in Beluran and Telupid on Sabah's east coast, is the latest community to secure a permanent presence at the cultural village, a living museum that showcases the heritage of Sabah's indigenous peoples.
While several indigenous communities continue to build temporary structures during the month-long Kaamatan celebrations in May to showcase their traditions, the permanent spaces provide a lasting presence.
Tens of thousands of visitors throng the village annually for the festival.
Today marks the fourth visit by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim to the festival since taking office in 2022.
Community leader Janai @ Tuni Sikawa @ Sikua said the new facility allows visitors to experience the Dusun Labuk way of life through interactive cultural activities and exhibitions.
Among the key attractions is a miniature tongkulabang, a unique padi-drying hut named after its butterfly-shaped roof design that can be opened and closed.
"We will also showcase Magalai Labuk traditional dance performances, traditional musical instruments and Sabung Labuk traditional attire known for its colourful bunga gosing embroidery.
"Visitors may also take part in hands-on activities such as learning traditional embroidery techniques, joining traditional dances and experiencing Dusun Labuk music," he said, adding that they produce fast rhythmic beats known as "titik tungkil" using traditional instruments such as gongs and kulintangan.
Accompanying the music is the Magalai Labuk dance, which is inspired by the graceful movements of the eagle, locally known as koniu, and performed with rhythmic foot stomping.
"Many community elders will be present at Valai Dusun Labuk, allowing visitors to hear and learn the language firsthand," he said, noting that the language is at risk of disappearing due to
linguistic evolution
among youths.
Traditional food, largely based on pickled or grilled freshwater fish, alongside traditional beverages, will also be served at the house.
Telupid assemblyman Datuk Jonnybone Kurum, who secured RM150,000 in government funding for the project, said the establishment represents an important recognition for the community.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Who gets priority on a jogging track? KLCC Park debate heats up
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A heated rant over people walking on the jogging track at KLCC Park has sparked debate on Threads.
The original post, which has since been deleted, appeared to show a family walking on the rubberised track, despite a pedestrian patterned footpath running next to it.
The Threads user, in the post, criticised the family's action, drawing backlash from other social media users.
However, the post also opened a wider debate on whether walkers should avoid using jogging tracks in public parks, especially when separate footpaths are available.
User jonnyyeong, who shared a screenshot of the deleted post, disagreed with the rant.
"Stupid post by an entitled person.
"KLCC Park is for everyone — walkers, joggers, strollers, runners, tourists and locals. This is not a track stadium.
"Don't upload photos of people who are just minding their own business and are just trying to have a brisk walk. They did you no harm," he wrote.
His post received more than 700 likes at the time of writing, with many users agreeing that public parks should remain shared spaces.
Some also argued that there were no signboards indicating whether the track was reserved exclusively for joggers or runners.
However, others said the point raised in the deleted post was not entirely wrong, even if the language was harsh.
One user, zvrytaah, said walkers should keep to one side and avoid blocking the jogging lane.
"I am not a jogger or runner, but I disagree with you. Walkers, please walk on one side and do not hog the whole lane.
"There are two lanes clearly separated there. Walkers should use the stone walkway, while joggers and runners use the running lane. This is common sense," the user wrote.
The user also said children running unpredictably on the jogging track could pose a risk to both walkers and joggers.
Several other users shared similar views, saying public spaces should be used with consideration for others.
The New Straits Times has reached out to KLCC Sdn Bhd for comments but there has been no response at the time of writing.
The official KLCC website lists both a jogging track and patterned footpaths among the facilities available at the 20ha park, a distinction that has become the centre of debate among social media users.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unrest outside US immigration detention centre, 9 arrested
Article:
NEWARK: Protesters clashed with US law enforcement outside an immigration detention centre in New Jersey, a top US official said Friday, after inmates inside staged a hunger strike over conditions.
Days of unrest outside the Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) detention centre in Newark have led to several arrests as a tough stance by US President Donald Trump's administration draws stiff opposition.
US Secretary of Homeland Security Markwayne Mullin posted on X that on Thursday evening, "approximately 100 anti-ICE rioters gathered around the Delaney Hall ICE facility. Rioters bit, kicked, and punched law enforcement officers." He said nine people were arrested.
Footage from US media outlets showed scuffles between protesters and law enforcement, who used pepper spray.
Delaney Hall is a private, 1,000-bed facility used exclusively by ICE that has operated since 2025 as the Trump administration pressed its campaign to deport millions of illegal immigrants.
New Jersey, on the US East Coast, is a so-called "sanctuary state," voluntarily limiting its cooperation with federal immigration authorities.
The turmoil outside Delaney Hall began after detainees inside launched a hunger and labour strike to protest conditions.
A hand-written Spanish-language letter published this week by Cosecha, a group advocating for undocumented immigrants, and signed by 300 detainees, said that they are "detained without justification," lack proper medical care and get "poor food."
US Senator Cory Booker, a New Jersey Democrat who visited the facility on Wednesday and said he spoke to dozens of detainees, echoed those concerns.
"The majority of the people I spoke with have no criminal history – no charges, no convictions – related to the kind of violence Donald Trump promised Americans he would target," Booker said in a statement.
"Delaney Hall should be closed down," he added.
State Governor Mikie Sherrill said she was denied access on Monday. The New Jersey Department of Health was able to inspect the center's food service facilities on Thursday but was denied full access, the Democratic governor said on X.
"Refusing to provide full access raises serious questions about what ICE is trying to hide from public view," she added.
Mullin responded to her on X, saying ICE detention centres are regularly inspected and audited by "external agencies."
"All detainees are provided with proper meals, quality water, blankets, medical treatment, and have opportunities to communicate with their family members and lawyers," he said. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WHO chief meets health, humanitarian partners in DR Congo as Ebola cases mount
Article:
KIGALI, (Rwanda): At least 134 confirmed cases of Ebola have been reported in the current outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda, according to an update Friday from the World Health Organisation (WHO), Anadolu Ajansi reported.
The report indicated the confirmed cases included nine in Uganda, and a doctor from the US, who had treated patients in Congo and is currently receiving care in Germany.
Deaths from Ebola stood at 18 among the confirmed cases in the two countries, the report showed, as WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus met health and humanitarian partners operating in Congo to discuss the response to the outbreak caused by the Bundibugyo virus in the country's east.
"We all agreed on the importance of strengthened coordination and collaboration among all partners, in support of the government and communities in Ituri Province, which is most affected by the outbreak," Tedros wrote on US social media company X after the meeting.
Congolese health authorities indicated that new suspected cases continue to be registered with a cumulative number of more than 1,000 recorded since the outbreak was declared May 15.
"We also discussed the power of working with community networks, from religious, business and women's groups, to radio and social media influencers, to communicate effective messaging with local people to build trust in measures that can protect everyone from the virus and help contain its spread," added Tedros, who arrived Thursday in Congo.
The Bundibugyo strain of Ebola, concentrated in the three eastern Congolese provinces including Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu, carries a fatality rate of between 30 per cent and 50 per cent among confirmed cases, according to the WHO.
Authorities have cited contact tracing and follow-up, insecurity, inadequate isolation, care and referral systems for patients among the challenges complicating response efforts.
Since the current outbreak, health authorities reported that a 32-year-old laboratory staff member who contracted Ebola was the first to be cured and discharged after a successful treatment at the Rwampara Hospital in Ituri province. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says making final decision on Iran deal
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said that he was making his decision on a potential deal with Iran, though Teheran insisted there was still "no final agreement" on ending the Middle East war.
A report from Iran's Fars news agency also rebutted several key elements of Trump's characterisation of the deal, citing informed sources as calling his remarks a "mixture of truth and lies."
US sources had told AFP the deal was just waiting on Trump's sign-off following weeks of halting negotiations to end a conflict that has engulfed the Middle East and shaken the global economy.
Trump attended a two-hour meeting in the White House Situation Room on Friday, but did not reach a decision on any new deal with Iran, The New York Times reported.
Trump announced the meeting in a lengthy social media post, reiterating long-held demands that Iran agree never to have nuclear weapons and must open the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping lane.
Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei, however, told state media that the Islamic republic "said goodbye to the language of 'must' 47 years ago."
"Regarding the understanding...exchanges of messages are continuing, but no final agreement has been reached yet," he added.
In his post, Trump said Teheran would remove mines in the Strait of Hormuz and end its blockade of the waterway with "no tolls," while the US would lift its parallel blockade of Iranian ports.
The two countries would coordinate on removing and destroying Iran's enriched uranium, he said, adding that "no money will be exchanged, until further notice."
Fars, however, cited Iranian sources as saying that Teheran was demanding "the immediate release of US$12 billion in frozen Iranian assets" and that "until this payment is made, Iran will not move to the next phase of negotiations."
As for the toll-free reopening of Hormuz, the sources said, "no such clause appears in the text of the agreement," while the comment on destroying Iran's nuclear material "is fundamentally baseless."
Baqaei also told state TV that there were currently "no negotiations" taking place on Iran's nuclear programme.
Trump's post came as Iran's top diplomat suggested the US was holding up a deal with its approach to the negotiations.
In a call with his Omani counterpart, Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the US must drop "excessive demands and shifting and contradictory positions," his ministry said.
Earlier Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, who led his country's delegation at talks with the US in Pakistan last month, said on X that Teheran placed "no trust in guarantees or words; only actions matter.
Sources have previously told Iranian media that any agreement unilaterally announced by Trump would not be recognized.
Ali, a resident of the city of Tonekabon north of Teheran, said that whatever the deal was, there would likely be more strife to come.
"Both sides are speaking in a way that keeps their supporters satisfied. It's not clear who is telling the truth," the 49-year-old said.
"If a deal is reached, the internal power networks will likely start a fight against each other and against opponents more than before."
Hopes of an agreement had risen on Thursday after US officials voiced optimism about the diplomatic progress.
Energy markets have whipsawed this week as investors parse the chances of an agreement that could potentially resume normal shipping through the crucial Strait of Hormuz.
Washington and Teheran have accused each other of violating the truce in and around the strait as recently as this week, with US strikes on the southern Iranian port of Bandar Abbas countered by retaliatory Iranian fire.
Iranian state TV said Friday that 24 ships had transited the strait in the past 24 hours, in coordination with the Revolutionary Guards and the foreign ministry.
But it warned that "ships from hostile countries face a severe response" from Iran's military.
On the war's Lebanon front, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Friday that his country's forces had pushed deeper inside Lebanon, while Iran-backed Lebanese group Hizbollah claimed responsibility for a series of drone attacks on military targets in northern Israel, including troop gatherings and a barracks.
The attacks came as Israeli and Lebanese military delegations were holding security talks in Washington.
Netanyahu said troops had crossed the Litani River, around 30km (20 miles) north of the Lebanon-Israel frontier, and were "hitting Hizbollah head on."
Israel also kept up its heavy bombardment of south Lebanon.
A ceasefire between Israel and Hizbollah was supposed to have taken effect on April 17, but has never been observed.
Both sides accuse each other of violating it and justify their attacks by the other camp's alleged breaches.
Lebanon was drawn into the war in early March when Hizbollah launched rockets at Israel over the killing of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli attacks, prompting Israeli strikes and a ground invasion. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vincent Tan seeks buyer for 30pct Prudential Malaysia stake [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Berjaya Group founder Tan Sri Vincent Tan is exploring a sale of his remaining 30 per cent stake in Prudential Assurance Malaysia Bhd (Prudential Malaysia), in a deal that could rank among the largest insurance transactions in Malaysia in recent years, people familiar with the matter said.
Sources also confirmed that the proposed sale could value the insurer at slightly above RM20 billion, significantly higher than the roughly RM8 billion valuation implied less than a year ago when Prudential Plc increased its ownership in the Malaysian business.
Financial advisers acting for Tan have begun preliminary discussions with a handful of potential investors, although the process remains at an early stage and no final decision has been made, one source familiar with the matter told Business Times.
"It is still in the early stages. Prudential Malaysia is performing strongly, and Tan believes the timing is right to monetise the investment," the source said, adding that the sale is expected to attract interest from deep-pocketed Malaysian investors and institutions, given the regulatory requirement that domestic insurers remain majority-locally owned.
Under Malaysian rules, foreign ownership in insurers is capped at 70 per cent, meaning any buyer for Tan's stake would need to be Malaysian.
The planned divestment follows a landmark restructuring completed last year involving Tan's investment vehicle, Detik Ria Sdn Bhd, which is linked to members of Johor's royal family, and UK-based insurer Prudential Plc.
The two parties resolved a long-running legal dispute that paved the way for Prudential's wholly owned subsidiary, Prudential Corp Holdings Ltd (PCHL), to increase its stake in Prudential Malaysia from 51 per cent to the maximum permitted 70 per cent.
Under the agreement, PCHL acquired an additional 19 per cent stake in Sri Han Suria Sdn Bhd, the holding company for Prudential Malaysia, from Detik Ria for RM1.52 billion. The transaction implied an enterprise valuation of around RM8 billion for the Malaysian insurer.
Following the transaction, Detik Ria retained a 30 per cent stake and secured an arrangement under which PCHL agreed to cooperate in the eventual divestment of the remaining interest to one or more mutually agreed Malaysian investors, should Tan opt to exit.
Prudential Malaysia is one of the country's largest life insurers and forms part of Prudential Plc's Malaysian life insurance segment, alongside its stake in Prudential BSN Takaful Bhd, one of Malaysia's leading takaful operators.
The insurer did not respond to requests for comment at the time of writing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IMF, World Bank, IEA chiefs warn of summer fuel scarcity if Hormuz strait remains closed
Article:
WASHINGTON: The heads of the International Monetary Fund, World Bank and International Energy Agency warned on Friday of the risks to fuel security during peak demand summer months if oil shipping through the Strait of Hormuz does not return to normal.
"Global oil inventories are being drawn down at a record pace in response to the major loss of supply through the Strait of Hormuz," the heads of the agencies said in a joint statement.
"If shipping flows do not return to normal, continued rapid depletion of global oil inventories ahead of peak summer oil demand in the Northern Hemisphere would present increasing risks for fuel security, market conditions, and broader economic resilience."
The US-Israel war on Iran has engulfed the Middle East in conflict, with Teheran's retaliatory action targeting Washington's regional allies and virtually blocking the key waterway, through which a fifth of global energy supplies normally pass.
In April, the heads of the IMF, World Bank and IEA announced they were forming a group to coordinate the agencies' response to the crisis, particularly for vulnerable economies.
In Friday's joint statement, they again highlighted that the surge in energy and fertiliser prices due to the war was having a disproportionate effect on lower income countries.
"Higher fertiliser prices are of particular concern as many countries enter the planting season," they said.
At the IMF's Spring Meetings this year, the organisation's chief Kristalina Georgieva highlighted that the war had forced a paring of the global growth forecast.
She estimated that vulnerable economies would need between US$20-50 billion in financial assistance due to the economic fallout of the conflict.
This week, the Fund announced that Bangladesh had requested a financial assistance package and that it was in talks to develop a programme to help the South Asian country.
The war has had broad-ranging impacts around the world, especially on countries that are heavily dependent on oil and gas imports from the Gulf, which includes much of south and southeast Asia.
Fertiliser supplies have also been impacted, and countries dependent on imports have been hit hard, with food security a key concern. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Umno holds edge in possible state polls'
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Umno's confidence ahead of rumoured state elections may be valid due to the Bersatu-Pas fracture and the launch of Bersama by former PKR members Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli and Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad, say analysts.
Merdeka Centre co-founder and programmes director Ibrahim Suffian said current circumstances could drive the party's momentum ahead of the possible Melaka and Johor polls.
With intensifying PKR-Bersama rivalry and a potential Bersatu-Pas fallout, Ibrahim said Umno could be a "stabilising force" for its federal counterparts while it re-establishes itself at the grassroots level.
He said the party's history with grassroots networks, loyal voters and strong leadership under Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi reflected its confidence, despite having lost seats to Perikatan Nasional previously.
"This environment is uniquely beneficial for Umno ahead of the next general election. The severe fragmentation of the Malay electorate across multiple competing parties naturally works to the advantage of an established incumbent," said Ibrahim.
He said an early call for elections could also give Umno a strategic advantage as voters would focus more on local issues rather than anger towards the federal government.
[[nid:1451294]]
Echoing this view, ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute senior fellow and coordinator of the Malaysia Studies Programme Dr Lee Hwok Aun said Umno was banking on reasonable voter satisfaction with the state government.
"Umno-BN is in the Johor government on its own. Johor is Umno's birthplace and still its bastion, enjoying voter goodwill compared to other states," he said.
Following former vice-president Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin's exit from Bersatu and fracture in the party's ties with Pas, the Malay political landscape had become more fragmented, improving Umno's chances in the polls, he said.
While Umno sees itself leading the Johor election on its own, Ibrahim also argued that separate state elections would usually see lower voter turnout by 10 to 15 per cent compared with general elections, particularly among minority groups.
He said Umno's confidence could be affected by questions over the nomination of a new prime minister.
The analysts said the rumoured state elections could see Umno secure a majority of seats in the state assembly.
ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute senior fellow and coordinator of the Regional Economic Studies and Malaysia Studies Programmes Dr Francis Hutchinson, however, said combining state and general elections could weaken its chances, as Pakatan Harapan voters were likely to "come out in full force" for parliamentary seat contests.
"We saw this in 2022, when Umno did really well in the (March) state election, but underperformed in the general election later that year," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Total vape ban may fuel black market, warns criminologist
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The use of "mushroom", "zombie" and "kuah" vape products among schoolchildren should be treated as a serious public health and criminological issue rather than dismissed as just moral panic.
Senior criminologist Datuk Dr P. Sundramoorthy said the trend reflected a broader shift in youth risk behaviour, illicit markets and digital consumer culture.
The Asian Criminological Society board member said the growing attraction to stronger vape substances among minors showed how deviant subcultures could evolve rapidly when demand, peer influence, curiosity and weak regulation intersected.
"The problem is especially worrying because many children do not fully know what they are inhaling, while social media platforms normalise vaping as fashionable, harmless and entertaining," he told the New Straits Times.
However, he cautioned against a total vape ban, saying prohibition alone might not offer the best long-term solution.
Criminological research consistently showed that outright bans often pushed markets underground, creating black-market networks that were harder to monitor and regulate, he said.
"This may unintentionally strengthen organised smuggling groups, illicit online sellers and cross-border syndicates already involved in distributing unregulated substances.
[[nid:1451291]]
"Prohibition alone can create hidden economies that are more dangerous because products become untraceable, unregulated and potentially more harmful."
Citing Singapore as an example, Sundramoorthy said the republic's strict prohibition on vape products and accessories had not eliminated illegal distribution activities.
"Despite aggressive enforcement, large quantities of vape devices are still regularly seized, while underground online sales continue to operate.
"This demonstrates an important criminological reality: prohibition by itself does not eliminate demand. Instead, it can shift the market into illicit networks where products become even less regulated and potentially more dangerous, particularly when criminal syndicates exploit youth demand for profit."
Instead, he called for stronger enforcement, tighter regulation and better monitoring.
Enforcement agencies should intensify operations against suppliers selling drug-laced vape products based on actionable intelligence, particularly against those targeting schoolchildren through online platforms and delivery services, he said.
He also proposed stricter licensing requirements, tighter import controls, mandatory product testing and heavier penalties for businesses or individuals caught selling vape products to minors.
Schools should work closely with police, health authorities and parents to identify early warning signs without unnecessarily criminalising children, he said.
He stressed that sustained public education and awareness campaigns were equally important, as many youths were introduced to vaping through curiosity, peer pressure or exposure at home.
"Some genuinely believe vaping is harmless compared to smoking. Preventive education must begin early at both primary and secondary school levels.
"Campaigns should move beyond fear-based messaging and instead provide scientifically-grounded explanations about nicotine addiction, psychological dependency, manipulation by online marketing and the unknown substances increasingly found in illegal vape liquids."
Parents also need to recognise how their own behaviour at home could influence children's attitudes towards vaping, he added.
"A balanced national strategy combining strong enforcement, effective regulation, digital monitoring and long-term education is likely to be far more effective than relying solely on prohibition.
"Malaysia must avoid simplistic solutions and instead adopt a practical, evidence-based approach that protects young people while preventing the expansion of dangerous underground markets," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police, schools may jointly carry out checks in classes for vapes
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Penang police are considering working with schools to jointly check students' belongings for vape products to keep them out of the children's hands.
State police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail said the issue was discussed during the State Drug Eradication Action Council meeting chaired by Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow on April 6.
He said although the cops had yet to receive any reports on the use of drug-laced vape devices in schools, they were continuously carrying out enforcement operations.
This, he said, included engagement sessions with local communities as well as state Education Department officials on the dangers of synthetic drug-laced vape products.
"To date, officers from the Narcotics Criminal Investigation Department together with school liaison officers, have conducted 34 talks on the dangers of vape abuse among students."
Each school in the state, he said, had been assigned two officers to strengthen crime prevention and disciplinary monitoring.
The officers conduct school visits at least once every two weeks, deliver talks on crime prevention, help with disciplinary issues, support urine screening tests conducted by the National Anti-Drugs Agency, and take part in inspections related to traffic offences as well as physical checks on students.
Police conducted Op Vape Bil 1/2026 with several agencies across Penang on April 13-17.
Compounds totalling RM49,800 were issued under the Control of Smoking Products for Public Health Act 2024 (Act 852) for various offences, including failure to register smoking products with the Health Ministry and promoting smoking products.
Police also seized vape liquids and devices worth RM4,951 and arrested a man under Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 after he tested positive for THC.
From January 2025 to this month, police recorded 115 cases and made 207 arrests linked to drug-laced vape products under various provisions of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 and the Poisons Act 1952.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel PM says forces pushing into Lebanon as delegations meet in US
Article:
BEIRUT: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Friday that his country's forces had pushed deeper inside Lebanon, even as Israeli and Lebanese military delegations were holding landmark security talks in Washington.
Israel also kept up its heavy bombardment of south Lebanon on Friday, with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun emphasising in a call with US Secretary of State Marco Rubio "the need to exert all efforts to reach a ceasefire" as an essential first step, his office said.
A ceasefire in Lebanon to halt fighting between Israel and the Iran-backed Hezbollah group was supposed to have taken effect on April 17, but has never been observed.
Both sides accuse each other of violating it and justify their attacks by the other's alleged breaches.
Israel and Lebanon, officially at war for decades, began direct talks in April, with a fourth round expected next week in Washington following Friday's meeting at the Pentagon.
A Lebanese military source told AFP the country's delegation would "emphasise the need for a ceasefire, and will present the army's plan for a state weapons monopoly and the extension of state authority across the country."
Hezbollah strongly opposes the talks and has refused to disarm.
'VERY DIFFICULT'
This week, Israel said it would ramp up operations in Lebanon, including on the ground in the south.
"Our forces have crossed the Litani," Netanyahu said during a visit to troops near the border, referring to the river that runs around 30 kilometres (20 miles) north of the Lebanon-Israel frontier.
"They have moved up to the commanding terrain. We are operating in Beirut, in the Bekaa, across the entire front and are hitting Hizbollah head on," he added, in a video released by his office.
On Friday, Lebanon's state-run National News Agency (NNA) reported Israeli airstrikes on more than 20 locations in the south, both before and after Israel's military issued evacuation warnings for eight towns, sparking a wave of displacement as people fled.
Hundreds of people have recently fled to the usually touristic old city of Tyre, which has been spared recent Israeli army evacuation orders for swathes of the rest of the city and its surrounds.
With shelters full, some are sleeping in their cars or tents, an AFP correspondent said.
Karam Amin, 43, and seven family members have been sleeping in his clothing shop.
"I sorted out a shower... and we put mattresses on the floor," said Amin.
"The situation is very difficult. Tyre is a peaceful, touristic city. We never imagined going through this," he added.
'SERIOUS DANGER'
Lebanon's health ministry said Israeli attacks have killed more than 3,355 people since the war began on March 2 – an increase of 31 compared to Thursday, which saw broad attacks on the south and the first airstrike near Beirut in weeks.
The UN children's agency UNICEF said 15 children had been killed over the past week, with 55 killed since the ceasefire announcement.
This week, Israel's military declared all areas south of the Zahrani – an area that includes the cities of Tyre and Nabatieh – as "combat zones" and told residents to evacuate.
Hezbollah also claimed several attacks on Israeli targets on Friday, including with drones.
The NNA said Israeli troops reached the outskirts of the southern town of Debbine overnight, while an AFP correspondent saw Israeli tanks between there and neighbouring Christian-majority Marjayoun.
Lebanon's Culture Minister Ghassan Salame told AFP on Friday that Israeli strikes on the country's south were putting heritage sites including in Tyre in "serious danger."
Lebanon's military delegation to Friday's talks includes six officers, headed by the army's director of operations, Georges Rizkallah.
On the Israeli side, Brigadier General Amichai Levin, head of the strategic division within the army's planning directorate, is in Washington for the talks, an Israeli military spokesman said.
Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the Middle East war with rocket fire at Israel over the killing of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli attacks, prompting Israeli strikes and a ground invasion.
Iran has insisted any agreement to end the broader Middle East conflict must apply to Lebanon. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teachers need help to clamp down on vape menace
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Vaping has become a full-blown crisis in schools, with nearly 20,000 student cases recorded nationwide in 2024 alone.
Parent Action Group for Education chairman Datin Noor Azimah Abdul Rahim said the alarming figures, based on Education Ministry data presented in Parliament, reflected how deeply vaping had penetrated schools.
She said concerns over youth vaping were particularly acute in Johor, Kedah, Kelantan, Terengganu and Perlis, where authorities had either debated or moved to restrict vape sales amid growing public concern.
"Teachers are concerned because vaping is now far more common and harder to control than traditional smoking.
[[nid:1451278]]
"The devices are small and easy to conceal, while flavoured vapour makes them difficult to detect. Many students also view vaping as socially acceptable because of peer influence and what they are exposed to on social media," she told the New Straits Times.
Noor Azimah said what was once viewed as an isolated disciplinary issue had evolved into a widespread problem affecting schools nationwide.
Teachers had observed behavioural changes among students who vape, including frequent gatherings in school toilets and lack of concentration during lessons, she said.
Schools were responding by conducting counselling and spot checks, confiscating vape devices and engaging with parents.
However, she warned that educators were increasingly overwhelmed by the scale of the problem.
"Teachers can only do so much. Schools should not be expected to shoulder the responsibility alone," she added.
Among the key challenges were increasingly sophisticated devices that were easier to hide, flavoured vapours that mask detection, easy online access to vape products and a lack of parental cooperation in some cases.
Educators were calling for a stronger whole-of-government approach, including stricter enforcement against vape sales to minors, tighter regulation of online sales and bans on vape shops near schools, she said.
"There must also be greater parental accountability because this issue cannot be addressed effectively without support from families," Noor Azimah added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Drug-laced vape use mushrooming [WATCH]
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: THE use of drug-laced vapes known as "mushroom", "zombie" and the latest craze, "kuah", is spreading among schoolchildren nationwide, with addiction among minors at an "alarming" level.
Consumers' Association of Penang's (CAP) anti-smoking activist, N. V. Subbarow, said the problem was most severe in Perlis, Kedah, Kelantan and Johor, with Penang fast emerging as another hotspot.
"Perlis, Kedah and Kelantan are among the worst-hit because of easy access and cross-border influence, while Johor is also experiencing a serious problem," he told the New Straits Times.
He claimed that students were moving beyond conventional fruit-flavoured vape products in their search of more intense highs.
Subbarow also warned that the latest vape products entering the underground market were becoming increasingly dangerous, with some allegedly containing drugs or unidentified substances.
He said the underground market was targeting schoolchildren and blamed this on weak enforcement.
"Vape products are packaged and marketed in ways that appeal to children. They can easily purchase them from their neighbourhood stores, which would keep these products under the counter and sell only to regular customers."
Subbarow added that suppliers openly marketed such products through social media and messaging apps, while online purchases remained largely unchecked.
"Customers can order online today and receive it tomorrow. Parents think the parcel contains food or ordinary items.
"Initially, the children were attracted to the fruity flavours they thought were harmless.
"But now they want something stronger for the excitement and thrill. Previously, there was the 'zombie' but now, we have the 'kuah', which is a combination of various flavours and more dangerous substances.
"This is turning our children into addicts and killing them."
He said the police were confident some of the confiscated vape products contained drugs.
"The bigger question is, where are these products coming from and how are the suppliers operating openly?"
Last month, Kuala Lumpur police foiled an attempt to introduce fentanyl, the deadly synthetic drug often dubbed the "zombie drug", into Malaysia after seizing 400 vape cartridges during coordinated raids in the Klang Valley.
They were among more than half a tonne of drugs worth over RM31.5 million seized.
BEHAVIOURIAL CHANGE
Subarrow said he had been receiving calls from concerned teachers and parents almost daily over the worsening vaping problem.
Subarrow said teachers and parents across several states had reported drastic behavioural change among students believed to be addicted to vaping.
"I received a call recently from a parent who told me his son collapsed after vaping.
"Some teachers and parents have told me that students and children who used to be respectful and hardworking are now withdrawn, aggressive and living in their own world."
According to Subbarow, the number of students caught vaping in schools has surged sharply over the past year.
"I visited one school in George Town about a year ago and there were about 50 students who vaped. I was informed by teachers that the number has now doubled.
"This is just one school. Many schools have also seen a 100 per cent increase in vaping among students."
He estimated that vaping among students had risen by as much as 50 per cent in many schools nationwide, with some users as young as 9 years old.
"In primary schools, many children admitted they tried vaping by using devices belonging to their parents or older siblings.
"They said they just wanted to cuba (try)," he said. "Some children even asked me where to get 'something stronger' when I met them at schools."
He said currently, there was the "kuah" trend.
"Before this, it was 'mushroom' and 'zombie'. Every few months, something new enters the market."
Some students were also modifying vape liquids themselves or requesting stronger mixtures from suppliers, he added.
"What they want is a stronger high. Some said they felt like they were flying after vaping."
He added that vaping had become increasingly normalised among students, with devices shared among group members to reduce costs.
"It is no longer individual use. Four or five students will share one vape device."
Schools were struggling to contain the problem, with some teachers also worried about confrontations during spot checks, he said.
"Students hide vape devices inside their clothing, underwear or school bags. Teachers are afraid because some students become unpredictable and difficult," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-Trump attorney-general Pam Bondi defends Epstein files release
Article:
WASHINGTON: Former US attorney-general Pam Bondi on Friday defended the Justice Department's handling of its files on sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein, while acknowledging "redaction errors" in a release that critics say shielded key information while identifying alleged victims.
Bondi was giving evidence to the House Oversight Committee in a transcribed interview rather than a sworn, videotaped deposition, frustrating Democrats and survivors who accuse President Donald Trump's administration of withholding the full record of the disgraced financier's sex trafficking case.
According to prepared remarks obtained by multiple US media outlets, Bondi said the Justice Department had made an "unprecedented commitment to transparency" under her leadership and produced nearly three million pages of material, including videos and images.
"To the best of my knowledge, the Department produced everything required under the Epstein Files Transparency Act," Bondi said in the statement.
But she also acknowledged flaws in the release.
"There were redaction errors," Bondi said, referring to the process of blacking out legally sensitive or private personal information. "But since day one of this process, this department has been committed to accountability and transparency."
The comments came as Democrats, Epstein survivors and some Republicans accuse the Trump administration of covering up the full record of the case, despite the Justice Department's insistence that it has released everything it is legally obliged to make public.
Bondi became a central figure in the Epstein saga after saying last year that the late sex offender's so-called client list was on her desk for review. The Justice Department and FBI later said there was no such list and no plans to release further information.
In her prepared statement, Bondi said she did not personally lead every part of the review and had delegated oversight to then-deputy attorney general Todd Blanche, now acting attorney general.
The Epstein Files Transparency Act required the Justice Department to remove names and identifying information of victims before releasing records, but barred officials from shielding powerful figures mentioned in the files merely because of embarrassment.
'NO MORE LIES'
Critics say the department failed on both fronts: it released names and photos of victims who had not previously been publicly identified, while continuing to redact other information that lawmakers argue should have been disclosed.
Bondi expressed sympathy for Epstein's victims, saying she was "deeply sorry" for what they had endured "as a result of that monster."
But survivors gathered outside the hearing room as Bondi entered and called for witnesses in the Epstein investigation to testify under oath and on camera.
The wider controversy has dogged Trump for much of his second term. Trump fired Bondi in April as frustration mounted over her handling of the Epstein files, although she was later named to a presidential science and technology council.
Lawmakers on the Oversight Committee voted in March to subpoena Bondi as part of their Epstein investigation, an unusual rebuke of a sitting Trump administration official by members of the president's own party.
But the committee's Republican chairman, James Comer, later shifted her appearance from a deposition to a transcribed interview – meaning it will not be videotaped or conducted under oath.
Top Oversight Democrat Robert Garcia came out of the session to accuse the government of continuing a cover up to protect Trump, who socialized extensively with Epstein for years before the pair were estranged in the mid-2000s.
Garcia said Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon, who accompanied Bondi despite still serving at the Justice Department (DOJ), had intervened repeatedly to stonewall questions about the president's involvement with the case.
"So the DOJ is in there right now stopping questions about President Trump, and about what happened in the release of these files, and why so many survivors were doxxed, and their information released to the public," he told reporters.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: In China, AMD CEO Lisa Su is understated but Nvidia's Huang more razzmatazz
Article:
WHEN AMD CEO Lisa Su arrived in China last week just days after Nvidia's CEO left, she kept a much lower profile than Jensen Huang, who drew crowds in the centre of the capital as he posed for photos and ate local delicacies like Beijing-style soybean paste noodles.
Their contrasting approaches show two very different forms of corporate diplomacy around the world's most politically sensitive artificial intelligence chip market, where their fortunes have also been diverging.
Just a year ago at the annual Computex trade show in Taipei, Huang said Nvidia's market share in China had dropped to 50 per cent from 95 per cent due to US export controls.
Since then, that has effectively fallen to zero, he said this year, amid Beijing's push for self-reliance in advanced AI chips. Huang has previously estimated that China's AI chip market would be worth US$50 billion this year.
By contrast, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) commands four per cent of China's AI chip market, according to IDC, a research firm, and it has more routes into the country than AI accelerators, which are Nvidia's mainstay.
AMD can serve Chinese customers with central processing units (CPUs), consumer graphics processing units (GPUs), AI chipsets and programmable integrated circuits called FPGAs, said Lian Jye Su, chief analyst at Omdia, a tech research firm.
That gives AMD access to more types of system architecture as AI workloads spread beyond training large models into broader enterprise use, he said.
With the two US tech CEOs showing up in Taipei before the Computex event, their diverging China playbooks appear more pronounced, as both over the past week unveiled big investment plans in Taiwan, home to the world's largest contract chip manufacturing firm, TSMC.
Their contrasting approach to China also carries an unusual personal dimension: both hail from Taiwan and have said publicly that they are distant relatives.
AMD's announcement on its Taiwan investment followed a flurry of meetings that Su had in China last week, where she spoke at an AMD developer event and met customers and partners.
She also had a publicly announced meeting with Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng, who said Beijing welcomes companies including AMD to "seize the opportunities presented by China's development and deepen mutually beneficial cooperation."
Beijing, however, did not have a comparable senior-level meeting for Huang, who was in Beijing around the summit between China's Xi Jinping and US President Donald Trump on May 14 and 15.
[[nid:1451264]]
That silence stood out because Huang has been the more vocal of the two executives in arguing that US chip curbs risk handing China's AI market to domestic rivals like Huawei.
An adviser to multinational CEOs with business in China, who declined to be identified so they could discuss specific executives, said Su's lower-profile approach was better suited to the current geopolitical environment.
The adviser added that the politicisation of US-China commercial ties and volatility in public sentiment had raised reputational risks for global chip CEOs.
Su said last week that China accounts for about 20 per cent of AMD's revenue and remains important across PCs, gaming and some data-centre segments.
"Frankly, you look at the size of the market and the size of our portfolio, and we'll continue to partner very closely with our Chinese customers," she said.
While Huawei and other domestic chipmakers jump into the void left by Nvidia, AMD's Shanghai developer event on May 19 suggests the Santa Clara, California-based company is also looking to fill the gap.
During the event, Su promoted ROCm, AMD's open-source software stack, to Chinese developers at a time when many AI firms are seeking alternatives to Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem because the most advanced Nvidia hardware is hard to obtain.
However, there are signs that the opening remains limited for now. AMD's software ecosystem is far less mature than Nvidia's and US export controls also restrict the US firm from selling its most advanced AI chips to China.
A person familiar with AMD's China operation who was not authorised to speak to the media said it sold a significant number of AI chips to Alibaba last year but the Chinese firm had to devote substantial engineering resources to debugging and adapting them because AMD's software ecosystem was weaker than Nvidia's.
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Jaslinda left a note', says mountain guide
Article:
IPOH: An inscription left on the ground has become proof that missing female hiker Jaslinda Saludin, 49, had reached the summit of Gunung Batu Putih near Tapah.
A member of the Perak Forestry Mountain Guide (MGP) team, who wished to be known only as Hafiz, said he discovered the inscription when he arrived at the summit while serving as the rear guard, or 'sweeper', for the Trans Spencer Chapman hiking expedition.
"Yes, she (Jaslinda) reached the summit, but she did not leave any hint as to where she went or which trail she took. She only informed us that she had reached the top," he told Bernama when contacted today.
He said the inscription, which read "HAFIZ AMBIK GAMBAR AKAK DH SAMPAI" ("Hafiz, take a photo, I've reached the summit"), was left by the victim because her mobile phone battery had run out at an early stage of the hike.
"She probably reached the summit of Gunung Batu Putih at about 10am. I arrived at around 10.45am and noticed the inscription at about 11am. So, she wanted to let me know that she had made it to the summit," he said, adding that he subsequently took a photograph of the inscription using his mobile phone.
Earlier, an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), or drone, was deployed to facilitate the search and rescue (SAR) operation for
Jaslinda
, who has been missing since May 23 after hiking at the mountain.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department director Sayani Saidon said yesterday that the SAR operation would focus on the Sungai Ayer Busok area, believed to be the route taken by the missing hiker to obtain a water source.
Prior to that, Jaslinda was reported to have started the Trans Spencer Chapman hike with 13 other participants and two MGP guides at 2 am on May 23 via the Pos Gedung-Gunung Bah Gading-Gunung Batu Putih-Kuala Woh route.
She was last seen by an MGP guide at about 7.30am on May 24 while continuing her ascent to the summit of Gunung Batu Putih after stopping along the route with another hiker, Mohd Hanafi Neikmad, 41, who had reportedly experienced health problems during the hike.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perlis Mufti: Shooting rampaging livestock permissible in Islam, halal to eat
Article:
KANGAR: Halal livestock that are difficult or impossible to catch for slaughter are permitted to be shot first with a gun or arrow.
Perlis Mufti Datuk Dr Mohd Asri Zainul Abidin said using hunting dogs is also allowed in Islam.
However, he said one must recite Bismillah (in the name of God) before releasing an arrow, firing a shot or releasing a hunting dog.
"If the arrow, bullet, or bite kills the animal, it is permissible to be eaten.
"If it does not kill the animal, it must be slaughtered as usual," he said in a statement on social media today.
A video went viral showing Perlis Menteri Besar Abu Bakar Hamzah firing a shot at a sacrificial cow that was running amok during a korban (Islamic slaughter rites) programme by the Kuala Perlis state constituency yesterday.
He later clarified that he was forced to shoot the cow to prevent it from injuring anyone.
He said the cow had broken free from its rope.
The Kuala Perlis assemblyman said the shot, fired from a shotgun, was aimed only at the animal's leg before slaughtering was carried out as usual.
"We conducted a korban involving 25 cows. Only one broke free and ran amok, so we had to stop it to prevent any harm or danger to the workers or the public.
"The cow we shot did not die. I personally slaughtered 24 cows, and only this one was shot in the leg before being slaughtered.
"I fired the shot to prevent anything untoward, such as what happened elsewhere, where there were people gored to death," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Flights, hotels divide travel into haves, have-nots
Article:
Rising airfares and hotel rates are making budget-conscious Americans delay or cancel summer trips while wealthy travellers are holding fast to vacation plans despite higher prices.
The trend underscores a growing divide in what had been a resilient post-Covid-19 pandemic travel rebound, another example of a so-called "K-shaped" economy that is showing up across consumer markets such as groceries, dining out and apparel.
Rising inflation, especially in fuel costs pushed up by the United States war on Iran since February, has been a driving factor.
Overall, 45 per cent of Americans in a recent Deloitte survey said they already made travel plans for this summer, the lowest in the last six years.
The middle-income cohort of US$100,000 to US$199,000 had the biggest drop, to 37 per cent from 45 per cent the year before.
Speaking at a Bernstein conference on Wednesday, American Airlines chief executive officer (CEO) Robert Isom said there was "no doubt" demand had a K-shaped pattern, with higher-income travelers outpacing middle- and lower-income customers.
Still, he said travel was growing across income groups and leisure demand was "incredibly" strong.
Among low- and middle-income consumers still travelling, many are waiting longer to book in hopes that fares will fall.
Some flyers are forgoing international trips and choosing cheaper destinations closer to home.
Budget-conscious consumers are opting for driving or cruise packages, which include accommodations, food, activities and sometimes even flights in one flat price.
Airfare rose more than 20 per cent last month from a year earlier, according to US government data, and airlines are increasingly passing through higher fuel costs via fares and fees that disproportionately hit economy travellers.
Outbound international summer bookings are down a quarter year over year, data from host travel agency InteleTravel showed.
"There are people saying, 'Let me see how long I can wait to see if prices are going to drop'," said Peter Vlitas, a vice-president at Internova Travel Group.
That pullback is most visible at the back of the plane.
Premium cabin fares have risen far more modestly — about seven per cent compared with sharp increases in economy fares — making business and first-class price hikes relatively more bearable for affluent travellers, said InteleTravel CEO James Ferrara.
Recent college graduate and professional fencer Kamar Andreas, 24, said he had scrapped a trip to Spain from Atlanta this summer because airfare hovered around US$2,000. He was expecting to spend about US$1,000.
Andreas said there was some essential travel on the cards this year, like training in France, but even with those trips, he was waiting in hopes prices will drop.
"I've been trying to see if prices will fluctuate before I book because some prices are absurd."
Millions of Americans are still expected to travel over peak summer periods, reflecting a willingness to absorb rising costs. Deloitte data shows travellers plan to spend more on their longest summer trips this year, even as fewer households participate.
But robust headline travel numbers are masking the growing divide.
Travel agents and marketplaces said they can see the gap getting wider as people are forced to change the way they travel.
Travel agent Erika Bullock said about half of the travellers expected to join a group trip she planned to Bali and Thailand dropped out.
"I know the flights had a lot to do with it, but then also planning going to Thailand and Bali, it's not a cheap trip."
Economy hotel demand is softening, with some operators discounting to fill rooms, while higher-end hotels continue to see stronger growth.
"The young and underemployed are finding it increasingly difficult to gain a foothold," said Aran Ryan, director of industry studies at Tourism Economics, an Oxford Economics company. "But we're still seeing an outlook for positive travel growth this summer."
The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI chip workers are demanding more as company profits soar
Article:
Runaway profits and sky-high valuations for microchip companies have fuelled worker demands over pay packages in South Korea, raising the question: who profits from the artificial intelligence (AI) boom?
In the United States, some employees with stock options have made it rich and retired early, while in Asia chip engineers are now using their "immense" leverage over companies to get their way, say analysts.
After memory chip giant Samsung Electronics reached a deal with its biggest union over bonuses, averting a major strike, AFP looks at what the dispute might mean for the industry.
Rapid advances in AI systems since text generator ChatGPT's 2022 breakthrough have sparked a gold rush for tech companies.
Massive demand for the silicon components used in AI data centres — especially memory chips, which are in short supply — has sent revenues soaring for firms that design, produce and assemble them.
Samsung's value topped US$1 trillion this month, followed by South Korean rival SK hynix and US chipmaker Micron — newcomers to a previously exclusive club of around a dozen companies, nearly all American.
"An unprecedented wave of insatiable demand" for advanced memory chips has made SK hynix and its peers "an indispensable backbone of the global AI infrastructure build-out", said William Keating, head of semiconductor research firm Ingenuity.
In the US, employees often get stock options, a form of so-called "golden handcuffs" allowing workers to profit from share price gains over a set period of time.
Asia's huge chip sector "is more dominated by labour unions", said Neil Shah, co-founder of Counterpoint Research.
With Taiwan and South Korea home to most of the world's chipmaking talent pool, engineers there hold "immense" leverage, said Shah.
"This skilled labour force know they are indispensable, they are contributing to these larger margins," he added.
Under the union deal, about 60 per cent of Samsung's domestic workforce is eligible to receive a bonus of roughly US$370,000 this year, based on a market estimate of operating profit.
Workers at SK hynix received bonuses more than three times larger than those paid by Samsung last year, according to Samsung's union.
A Samsung strike "would almost certainly have been the biggest work stoppage in the history of the global semiconductor industry", said South Korean writer and researcher Kap Seol in an article for US magazine Jacobin.
In the chip world, "high pay and generous benefits often foster a sense of privilege and prestige" among workers, "despite their experience of chemically drenched working conditions, cutthroat competition and long, risky working hours", he wrote.
There have also been reports of discontent over bonuses at Taiwan's chip production giant TSMC, where AI demand has brought record profits.
"As the company continues to grow, we are highly confident that the full-year growth percentage of our employee profit-sharing... will surpass that of last year," said TSMC in a statement.
TSMC boss C.C. Wei held a meeting to explain the bonus situation to staff on Wednesday. The atmosphere was "calm and friendly", said a company spokesperson, adding that annual bonuses were set to grow "more than 30 per cent" year-on-year.
The Samsung agreement is fuelling labour demands in other sectors across South Korea — with workers in industries from biotech and autos to shipbuilding asking for a larger share of corporate profits through bonuses.
According to Shah, in general terms, company shareholders are reaping the most from booming profits, followed by senior company executives and then employees who have stock options.
Fourth are the chip engineers, some of whom are now demanding a greater share of the pie.
In the case of Californian AI chip titan Nvidia — the world's most valuable company at more than US$5 trillion — many employees with stock options became millionaires very quickly, said Shah.
"Many of them actually left and became investors" or simply chose to retire "on the beach".
The writers are from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: Sanctioning the death penalty
Article:
Since Merdeka in 1957, Malaysia has executed 469 people. The last convicts to be  brought to the gallows were in  2017, before the mandatory death penalty was abolished in 2023, granting judges  discretion in sentencing.
In Malaysia, you can  be hanged for  murder, drug trafficking, terrorism, treason and waging war, kidnapping for ransom, hostage taking, firing a gun when committing certain crimes and abetting a mutiny.
The death penalty has been the subject of  much debate and even public fascination. High-profile convicts sent to the gallows include Mona Fandey, her husband Affandi Abdul Rahman and an assistant.
The trio were executed in 2001 for the gruesome 1993 ritual murder and decapitation of politician Datuk Mazlan Idris.
Four  members of Al-Ma'unah, led by Mohamed Amin Mohamed Razali, were executed in August 2006 for "waging war" against the king during the 2000 Sauk Siege.
Wong Swee Chin, aka Botak Chin, was hanged for firearm-related offences rather than his notoriety for violence.
Ahmad Najib Aris was executed in 2016 for kidnapping, sexually assaulting and murdering Canny Ong, a crime that compelled shopping complexes to upgrade security in parking lots.
Brian Chambers and Kevin Barlow were the first Westerners to be executed in Malaysia for drug trafficking in 1986.
The death penalty emerged independently across early ancient civilisations.
The earliest known law that provides for the death penalty  may be traced to ancient Mesopotamia 6,000 years ago, documented in the Code of Hammurabi in Babylon.
The ancient Egyptians often beheaded the guilty or imposed forced suicide.   Following Malaysia's  landmark 2023  reform, prisoners facing capital punishment have had their sentences commuted to long-term imprisonment.
Suddenly, the death penalty debate is being  reignited after the Klang High Court sentenced a lorry driver to death for murdering a boy in 2021.
Judge Norliza Othman ruled that death by hanging was the only appropriate and proportionate punishment for S. Davandakumar for the brutal killing of K. Nithish. That sentence has rekindled the death penalty debate in Malaysia.
It's difficult to dissect the complexities when globally, executions for  murder persist despite concerns about wrongful convictions and the morality of executions. Yet, crimes of extreme cruelty, especially against children, challenges abolitionists' views while proponents argue that the issue is about "proportionality, not revenge".
Understandably, certain crimes are so heinous that a death penalty is imposed even though evidence of  a  deterrent effect is hard to gauge.
In the long term, policymakers have to construct a justice system both humane and credible. A system that protects the vulnerable but punishes abominable crimes proportionately and delivers justice effectively.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UN says protecting children online an 'urgent priority'
Article:
GENEVA: Making the digital world safe for children is an urgent priority, the United Nations said on Friday, adding that those responsible for online harm must be held to account.
UN rights chief Volker Türk said states had to force tech giants to embed child safety into their platforms, and said child harm was the direct result of business practices and design choices.
"The digital world that connects children to learning, community and creativity also exposes them to real risks to their safety, privacy and well-being," Türk said in a statement.
"Online harms in those fields "are not innate or inevitable; they result from design choices and business practices that undermine safety, including addictive design features such as infinite scroll, autoplay and persistent notifications from apps," he said.
"Enhancing protection of children online is an urgent priority that we need to make sure not only gets done – but that it gets done right."
In December,
Australia
became the first country to require TikTok, YouTube, Snapchat and other top sites to remove accounts held by under-16s or face heavy fines.
Indonesia has imposed a similar ban, while several European countries are looking at following suit.
In a submission to Britain's consultation on the issue, which ended this week, the Academy of Medical Royal Colleges said doctors were seeing a "wave of radicalised children" from exposure to "hateful, addictive and grossly distressing content".
"Blanket social media bans are not a one-off panacea," said Türk.
"Simply limiting access to platforms that remain unsafe cannot stand as the endpoint in effectively protecting children."
Micro-targeting of children
The UN high commissioner for human rights called for tougher measures by governments and tech firms to make online platforms safe for children through better design, data protection, regulation, oversight and accountability.
He called for action to ensure that "those responsible for harm can be held to account".
Türk said simply focusing on age restrictions would leave unaltered the designs and algorithms that made the platforms unsafe in the first place.
The UN rights chief also said experience so far showed that bans could be easily circumvented and voiced concern that such bans could even end up pushing children on to riskier, less monitored platforms.
The UN rights office produced a set of 10 guidelines entitled Getting Children's Safety Online Right.
These included ensuring the maximum protection of children's data as a default setting, while the "micro-targeting" of children for commercial purposes based on a digital record "should not be permitted".
They said emerging concerns such as restrictions on artificial intelligence chatbot use or addictive design features may warrant age restrictions.
Measures should be subject to independent oversight, with legal consequences that serve as a deterrent, the guidelines said.
There should also be access to remedy for children whose rights are violated.
No 'quick fix'
The UN rights office issued the guidelines as multiple countries are considering social media bans for children, while children and social media may feature on the G7 summit agenda next month.
"For too long, social platforms and apps have not met this responsibility by fully understanding the risks that platforms pose and taking the steps to address those harms," said Peggy Hicks, the UN human rights office's thematic and special procedures director.
"Often they've prioritised user expansion or engagement rather than the well-being of kids," she told a press conference in Geneva.
Hicks said there was no "quick fix" to the problem, but the guidelines should help ensure future measures are grounded in human rights and children's rights.
She said children themselves must be involved in the process, since they will often recognise online threats and problems before adults do.
Hicks said tech companies faced a choice: "change how their platforms are designed and operated to better protect children's rights and safety, or be forced to do so through increasingly restrictive legislation, jury verdicts and regulatory fines."
— AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US judge freezes Trump 'slush fund' for allies
Article:
WASHINGTON: A federal judge on Friday temporarily blocked the Trump administration from moving ahead with a US$1.8 billion compensation package that critics have denounced as a "slush fund" for President Donald Trump's political allies.
US District Judge Leonie Brinkema barred the administration from taking any further action to create or operate the fund while she considers whether to impose a longer-lasting pause.
Her order blocks the transfer of money into the fund, the consideration of claims and the disbursement of any payments, saying the freeze was needed to ensure that no money was "irreversibly disbursed" before the legal challenge is heard.
The decision is another setback for one of Trump's most politically explosive second-term initiatives, which has already alarmed Democrats, legal experts and some Republicans in Congress.
The fund was created by the Justice Department as part of an extraordinary settlement of Trump's civil lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service over the leak of his tax returns by a former government contractor.
The administration says the programme is intended to compensate people who suffered from government "weaponisation" and "lawfare" — Trump's terms for what he says was the politically motivated targeting of conservatives and his supporters.
But opponents say the fund has no clear legal basis, little public oversight and could be used to reward loyalists, including defendants convicted of crimes related to the 2021 assault on the US Capitol by Trump supporters.
Brinkema's brief order came after a lawsuit by a group of plaintiffs including Andrew Floyd, a former federal prosecutor who investigated and prosecuted Jan 6 Capitol riot defendants, and Jonathan Caravello, a California professor arrested while protesting an immigration raid.
They argued that the fund amounted to a "collusive agreement" between Trump and his administration, with "no congressional authorisation, no basis in law and no accountability".
The judge set a June 12 hearing to consider whether the government's work on the fund should remain frozen for longer.
— AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Body of jet ski operator found off Langkawi coast
Article:
ALOR STAR: The body of a man who was feared drowned after he was believed to have tried to save a drifting jet ski in an incident off Jalan Pantai Tengah, Langkawi yesterday, was found today.
Langkawi Zone 4 Fire and Rescue Department chief Mohd Zamri Abdul Ghani said the body of 23-year-old Arash Nashran Abdul Maser, the jet ski operator, was discovered at about 6pm by a search and rescue team conducting patrols at sea.
"The search operation yesterday continued until 2.39am (today) along the coastline, but had to be suspended due to rough seas and strong winds.
"The search resumed at 6.50am and three jet skis belonging to members of the public were used to locate the victim. At 6pm, the victim's body was found and brought to the jetty by boat," he said in a statement today.
He said at 6.48pm, Health Ministry (MOH) personnel confirmed that the victim had died before the body was handed over to the police for further action.
Yesterday, media reports stated that a man was
feared drowned
after he was believed to have tried to save a drifting jet ski at sea before strong waves caused him to be thrown into the water.
It was understood that the victim was not wearing a life jacket at the time.
Meanwhile, Langkawi District Police chief assistant commissioner Khairul Azhar Nuruddin said the body was found about 100m from the location where the victim was reported missing, and the case had been classified as a sudden death report (SDR).
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sultan of Pahang, Tengku Ampuan extend journalists' day greetings
Article:
KUANTAN: The Sultan of Pahang, Al-Sultan Abdullah Ri'ayatuddin Al-Mustafa Billah Shah, and the Tengku Ampuan of Pahang, Tunku Azizah Aminah Maimunah Iskandariah, have extended National Journalists' Day (Hawana) 2026 greetings to media practitioners and journalists across the country.
In a post on the Kesultanan Pahang Facebook page, the royal couple expressed appreciation for the dedication and sacrifices of journalists committed to delivering accurate, credible and trustworthy information to the public.
Their Royal Highnesses also highly valued the role of the media as an important platform for strengthening unity, fostering patriotism and serving as a bridge between the government and the people.
"Their Royal Highnesses hope that media practitioners will continue to uphold ethical, professional and responsible journalism in line with technological developments and the evolving challenges of today's communications landscape," the post read.
The royal couple prayed that all journalists and media practitioners would be blessed with well-being and safety, and continue to excel in carrying out their responsibilities to religion, nation and country.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WFH fuel savings 'marginal' as petrol demand keeps rising, says economist
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Petrol consumption continues to increase despite the government calling for work-from-home (WFH) arrangements last April, signalling the problem lies deeper beyond household behaviour.
Centre for Future Studies Bhd chief executive officer and chief economist Dr Mohd Yusof Saari said that Malaysia's petrol demand is driven by logistics, production, household mobility and energy-intensive sectors.
"Nationally, the impact is marginal," Yusof said in response to the WFH policy which the government placed in order to cushion the impact of rising fuel and essential goods prices caused by global tensions.
He added that private-sector workers, logistics operators, schools, small businesses, retailers and service providers still require physical mobility — groups not involved in the government's WFH policy.
"This means a large share of our economy depends directly or indirectly on energy use, whether through production, logistics, transport, wholesale and retail trade, manufacturing, food supply chains, services, or petroleum-linked activities."
In April, the government announced a
WFH policy within the public sector
as a way to reduce fuel consumption while facing global price shocks.
While it is said to involve approximately 200,000 civil servants, the private sector is not tied to the policy as decisions for remote working should come from the employers.
Households, Yusof said, continue to use petrol not because they are unwilling to change behaviour, but because the current economic and transport system still requires them to move.
He added while encouraging Malaysians to use public transport or work remotely can help reduce petrol usage, what is often overlooked is the response from production.
"A large share of fuel demand is generated by production, logistics, supply chains and business operations.
"The government must also target industrial upgrading, logistics optimisation, renewable energy adoption, and firm-level energy management.
On May 25, Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar said the WFH policy
led to savings
of 2.14 million litres of fuel and reduced subsidy expenditure by RM4.2 million since April 17.
However, Yusof said this amount is still relatively small compared to the size of national petrol consumption since the arrangement only applies to the public sector and mobility remains essential for workers.
"Many workers cannot work remotely.
"Parents still need to send children to school. Small businesses still depend on transport and delivery. Public transport coverage is still uneven, especially outside major urban corridors," he said.
To overcome the rising prices, he suggested the government adopt temporary household relief packages, particularly to vulnerable income groups and essential workers.
"This can be done through transport vouchers, targeted cash assistance, fuel-related assistance for eligible groups, and support for households with high commuting dependency," he added.
He said in the medium term, Malaysia should implement a more serious industrial energy efficiency programme to reduce energy consumption per unit of output including tax deductions, accelerated capital allowance, green technology grants, low-interest financing and performance-based incentives for firms.
Appearing on an episode of Keluar Sekejap, Prime Minister's Office senior economic and finance adviser
Nurhisham Hussein
said data showed petrol usage had slightly increased.
While oil prices are relatively stable in Malaysia, the global oil market's outlook is uncertain, especially in the third quarter of the year.
In addition to high consumption, Nurhisham also warned the economy is expected to be "very uncomfortable" by July and August as prices will accelerate even more, along with lingering supply shortages.
He said existing measures introduced by the government have yet to reduce overall fuel consumption.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Health Department investigates viral claim of delayed treatment at Tumpat Hospital
Article:
KOTA BARU: The state Health Department is investigating a viral social media claim alleging that a 17-year-old patient at Tumpat Hospital was subjected to delayed treatment on Wednesday.
Its director, Datuk Dr Mohd Azman Yacob, said the incident occurred at the hospital's Emergency and Trauma Unit.
He said the teenager arrived at the hospital at 9.50pm, complaining of shortness of breath, fever, cough and flu-like symptoms.
"She was examined by a medical officer two minutes later, and initial checks found no signs of an acute asthma attack.
"Further assessment at the Secondary Triage also found that the patient's condition was stable.
"She was then placed in the Green Zone while awaiting further examination as there were other cases in the Yellow and Red Zones requiring immediate treatment at the time," he said.
However, Dr Azman said that when hospital staff called the patient again for further examination at 12.02am, she was found to have left the hospital without informing staff.
"We acknowledge that there are several aspects that need improvement, particularly communication and patient management at the unit.
"I have directed Tumpat Hospital to conduct a further investigation and improve existing procedures to ensure more effective service delivery," he said.
As an immediate measure, he said patients with a history of asthma would be placed under continuous monitoring until treatment is completed.
"In addition, patients in the Green Zone awaiting further examination will be reassessed periodically while waiting for treatment," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Singaporean nurse killed in crash, falls from Johor Causeway flyover
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A 25-year-old Singaporean nurse was killed after his motorcycle crashed and he fell from a flyover along the Johor Causeway here yesterday.
In the 3.20am incident, the victim, who was riding a Yamaha NMAX, was travelling from Bukit Chagar towards the Sultan Iskandar Building.
Johor Baru Selatan police chief Assistant Commissioner Raub Selamat said the victim was believed to have mistakenly entered the car lane before attempting to turn back against traffic.
"Upon arriving at the scene, the victim is believed to have lost control before skidding to the right side of the road, hitting the road barrier and scraping along a sharp bend.
"The impact threw the victim off the motorcycle before he fell from the flyover, about 10m high.
"He was later found on the lorry lane from Singapore to Malaysia with severe head injuries, as well as wounds and scratches on several parts of his body," he said in a statement.
